explanation: The input natural language states that whenever 'not_Eclipse' is equal to 1, then 'sun_angle' shall be less than 45. In LTL, this can be modeled as a safety property: globally, if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' holds, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must also hold. This is represented as G (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). This translation ensures that at all times, whenever the condition on 'not_Eclipse' is met, the restriction on 'sun_angle' applies. It directly mirrors the structure of the requirement and does not rely on numerical comparison operators, only on atomic propositions.

The input specifies a requirement: If (not_Eclipse is equal to 1), then (sun_angle is less than 45). This is a safety property that should hold globally: Whenever not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 is true, sun_angle_lessThan_45 must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is captured by: G (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). Previous patterns that omit the global 'G' operator or misplace the implication would not enforce that the property must always hold throughout the entire execution.

The requirement states: "If the value of signal not_Eclipse is equal to 1, then the value of signal sun_angle shall be less than 45." We have the atomic propositions 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' and 'sun_angle_lessThan_45'. In LTL, to capture an implication that whenever 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is true, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must hold, we use the 'globally' operator for invariance. The correct translation is: G (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). This says that at all times, if not_Eclipse is 1, then sun_angle is less than 45. This captures the requirement accurately. Previous possible alternatives (such as ones using 'eventually' or 'until') do not capture the 'whenever' structure required here, as they might only require the sun_angle to become less than 45 at some later point. Only the invariant implication correctly and strictly enforces the requirement as a safety property.

The natural language requirement states: 'If the value of signal not_Eclipse is equal to 1, then the value of signal sun_angle shall be less than 45.' This is a safety property, specifying that whenever the condition 'not_Eclipse is equal to 1' holds, the condition 'sun angle is less than 45' must also hold at that point in time. In LTL, this is captured as 'globally, if not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 holds, then sun_angle_lessThan_45 must also hold.' The implication ensures that at every point in an execution, it is never the case that not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 holds without sun_angle_lessThan_45 holding as well. The property is thus: G (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). No numerical comparison operators are used in the LTL formula, and only the specified atomic propositions are referenced.

In linear temporal logic, this is most naturally encoded by the safety property: globally, if not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 holds, then sun_angle_lessThan_45 must also hold at the same time. This is expressed as G (not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). This LTL property ensures that at every point in time, if 'not_Eclipse' is equal to 1, then the required condition on 'sun_angle' holds. No previous LTL was given, so no comparison is necessary.

The natural language requirement says: If 'not_Eclipse' is equal to 1, then 'sun_angle' shall be less than 45. The definition of 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' is 'not Eclipse is equal to 1' (which acts as the antecedent), and 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' is 'sun angle is less than 45' (which acts as the consequent). In LTL, to express "if p then q whenever p holds" throughout the execution, we use globally (G) to enforce that at every point in time, if 'not_Eclipse_equalTo_1' holds, then 'sun_angle_lessThan_45' must hold as well. This is captured by G(not_Eclipse_equalTo_1 -> sun_angle_lessThan_45). This ensures there are no exceptional intervals where the antecedent is true and the consequent is false.
